title: Paris-Brest
subtitle: Choux ring filled with hazelnut praline cream
excerpt: A large ring of choux pastry, split and filled with a praline mousseline: a pastry cream beaten with softened butter and blended with a hazelnut praline made in the kitchen. The choux ring is scattered with flaked almonds before baking and dusted with icing sugar to finish. Named after the Paris to Brest cycling race, created in 1891 by the baker Louis Durand to commemorate the route.
sourceNotes: The Paris-Brest was created in 1891 by Louis Durand, a patissier in Maisons-Laffitte, at the request of Pierre Giffard who founded the Paris-Brest-Paris cycling race. The circular shape represents a bicycle wheel. It is composed of a choux ring (sometimes individual choux buns) filled with crème mousseline (pastry cream enriched with butter) flavoured with praline (a caramelised nut paste). The recipe here makes the praline from roasted hazelnuts and caramel, which is the correct traditional approach rather than using bought praline paste.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the praline up to 1 week ahead and store in an airtight container. Make the pastry cream a day ahead. Assemble on the day and refrigerate for 1 hour before serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 180°C. Same rules as éclairs: do not open the oven in the first 20 minutes. Reduce to 180°C conventional for the final 15 minutes to dry the ring through. Pierce the ring in several places and return to the oven for 5 minutes.

recipeTools.notes: large baking tray, lined with baking paper
fitted with a 1.5 cm plain or star nozzle
for beating the mousseline to a stable emulsion; a hand mixer is a workable substitute

glossaryTerms.definition: A pastry cream (crème pâtissière) enriched with softened butter beaten in after cooling, flavoured with hazelnut praline. The mousseline is lighter and richer than plain pastry cream. The key is temperature: the pastry cream must be the same temperature as the softened butter when they are combined, or the cream will split rather than emulsify. Beat the butter first until light, then add the cold pastry cream a spoonful at a time. Once the blended praline paste is stirred in, the mousseline should be thick enough to hold a piped shape.

content.text: Paris-Brest has three components made in sequence: the choux ring, the praline, and the

. The praline and pastry cream can both be made a day ahead. The critical moment is the mousseline: the pastry cream and butter must be at the same temperature before they are combined. If the cream is too cold the butter will seize into hard lumps; if too warm, the cream splits. The finished mousseline should be silky enough to pipe but firm enough to hold a shape.

attrs.prepNote: to scatter on top before baking

content.text: For the praline, toast the hazelnuts in a dry pan until golden. Rub in a clean tea towel to remove the skins. Cook the caster sugar and water in a saucepan without stirring until the caramel turns deep amber. Pour over the hazelnuts on a lined baking tray. Leave to set until cold and completely hard. Break into pieces and blitz in a food processor until the mixture first becomes a rough powder, then a smooth paste. Set aside.

Make the pastry cream: heat the milk until steaming. Whisk the yolks, sugar, flour, and cornflour together, pour in the hot milk, then return to the pan and cook until very thick. Add the vanilla. Press clingfilm to the surface and refrigerate until cold. Make the choux as for éclairs (bring water and butter to a boil, beat in flour, cool slightly, beat in eggs). Pipe a large ring about 22 cm in diameter onto a lined tray, then pipe a second ring immediately inside and a third ring on top. Scatter the flaked almonds over. Bake at 200°C for 20 minutes without opening the oven, then reduce to 180°C and bake for 15 to 20 minutes more. Pierce the ring several times and return for 5 minutes. Cool completely.

For the mousseline, beat the softened butter in a stand mixer until very pale and light. Add the cold pastry cream one spoonful at a time, beating well between each addition. Fold in the praline paste until evenly combined. Transfer to a piping bag. Split the choux ring horizontally. Pipe the mousseline generously into the base. Replace the top and dust with icing sugar. Refrigerate for 1 hour before serving.
